'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja (regional shrine)|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōja (regional shrine)|qq=}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|qq=}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shōen|qq=}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō River|qq=}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|lt=Water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji|qq=}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|qq=}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|qq=}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|qq=}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōsha|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsha|qq=}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manor|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manor|qq=}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shōgawa River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgawa River|qq=}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, both {{ill|Water Gods|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|qq=}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku era|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku era|qq=}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin|qq=}} from {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|qq=}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|qq=}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|qq=}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|qq=}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|qq=}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|1=Q45261123|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45261123}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} from {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神|1=Ugajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q795080}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōja (regional shrine)|1=Sōja shrine}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タイプ|1=Type|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2463013}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen|1=Manorialism}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shō River|1=Shō River}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|1=Kuraokami}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|1=Q45261123|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45261123}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} from {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神|1=Ugajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q795080}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

== References ==
* ''Shōgawa Chōshi Volume 1'', Shōgawa Town Historical Compilation Committee, 1975, pp. 122–130.

|deity= {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}
|type= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor)・{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}
}}

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja (regional shrine)|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōja (regional shrine)|qq=}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|qq=}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shōen|qq=}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō River|qq=}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|lt=Water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji|qq=}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|qq=}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|qq=}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|qq=}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.


== References ==

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōsha|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsha|qq=}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manor|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manor|qq=}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shōgawa River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgawa River|qq=}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, both {{ill|Water Gods|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|qq=}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku era|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku era|qq=}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin|qq=}} from {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|qq=}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|qq=}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|qq=}}. '''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōja (regional shrine)|1=Sōja shrine}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タイプ|1=Type|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2463013}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen|1=Manorialism}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shō River|1=Shō River}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|1=Kuraokami}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|1=Q45261123|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45261123}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} from {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神|1=Ugajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q795080}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

|deity={{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}}, {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}
|ShrineRank={{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small) · {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}
}}
'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|1=Q45261123|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45261123}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} from {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神|1=Ugajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q795080}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

== References ==
* ''Shōgawa Chōshi Volume 1'', Shōgawa Town Historical Compilation Committee, 1975, pp. 122–130.

|location= {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}} Shogawa-machi Sho 6446
36°35'29.4"N 136°59'49.3"E
|位置 = {{Coord|36|35|29.4|N|136|59|49.3|E|type:landmark_region:JP-16|display=inline,title}}
|deity= {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}
|type= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor)・{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}
}}

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja (regional shrine)|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōja (regional shrine)|qq=}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|qq=}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|shōen|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shōen|qq=}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō River|qq=}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|lt=Water deities|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku|qq=}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji|qq=}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|qq=}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|qq=}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|qq=}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami|ja|砺波市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami|qq=}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōsha|ja|総社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōsha|qq=}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manor|ja|荘園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manor|qq=}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shōgawa River|ja|庄川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgawa River|qq=}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|qq=}} and {{ill|Kuraokami no Kami|ja|闇龗神|1=Kuraokami|2=ja|3=淤加美神|4=de|5=Kuraokami|6=sv|7=Okami-no-Kami|8=zh|9=闇淤加美神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|qq=}}, both {{ill|Water Gods|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Water Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin|qq=}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=|1=Seoritsu-Hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kiryaku|qq=}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|qq=}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku era|ja|延暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku era|qq=}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsōin|qq=}} from {{ill|Tenpyō Hōji|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō Hōji|qq=}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|qq=}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|qq=}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|qq=}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|qq=}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|qq=}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|qq=}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|ja|高瀬神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takase Shrine|qq=}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōja (regional shrine)|1=Sōja shrine}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タイプ|1=Type|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2463013}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen|1=Manorialism}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shō River|1=Shō River}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|1=Kuraokami}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}} (formerly Shogawa Town), {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is also referred to as Shō-no-Miya or Ogami-sama. It serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōja (regional shrine)|1=Sōja shrine}} of Shoshita-go. Its former {{ill|Shakaku|ja|type|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タイプ|1=Type|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2463013}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.

The surrounding area of the shrine was historically known as "Ogami no Shō" (庄 refers to {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=shōen|1=Manorialism}} or manor), which gave the {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shō River|1=Shō River}} its name. Previously, the river was also called Ogami River, named after the shrine.

The shrine enshrines {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, specifically {{ill|Kuraokami|lt=Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuraokami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami no Kami|1=Kuraokami}}, as the main deities, with {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} as a subsidiary deity.

== History ==

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its first historical mention is in the ''{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} 14 (795), August 18, which notes that Takaokami, Ogami, and Futagami received the court Rank of ''Jūgoin-jō'' (Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade). An earlier map from ''{{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}}'' 3 (759), stored in the Shōsōin, includes details about land allocation between Ogami Shrine and {{ill|Kibunashi Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibunashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine is listed as one of the seven shrines in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}}. 

The name "Ogami" (雄神) is thought to contrast with "Hime Shrine" (姫神), listed just above in the ''Engishiki.'' It may refer to male deities or reflect the nature of the enshrined deities, although the association with the current deities is considered a later interpretation.

Originally, the shrine was located near the Shō River. In Kanbun 3 (1663), flooding caused the river to carve a new course between the main hall and the worship hall. Consequently, in Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position. The worship hall was repurposed to enshrine the same deities as the original shrine, becoming the former Ogami Shrine. Later, the subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ugajin|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ugajin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神}}, the Benzaiten Ugamiya, was merged into the former Ogami Shrine, which was renamed Benzaiten Shrine. This shrine opens for worship every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, when {{ill|Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōsō Buddhist monks|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} overthrew Ishikuro of Fukumitsu Castle, the neighboring {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} came under the control of the Kōsō sect. Some priests from Takase Shrine relocated to Ogami Shrine. To this day, the two shrines share a close relationship, with their chief priests serving both shrines concurrently.

'''Ogami Shrine''' (雄神神社, おがみじんじゃ) is a shrine located in {{ill|Tonami, Toyama|en|Tonami, Toyama|WD=Q526760|lt=Tonami|1=Tonami, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is commonly referred to as "Shō-no-Miya" or "Ogami-sama" and serves as the {{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|WD=Q1107129|lt=Sōsha|1=Sōja shrine}} of the Shō Lower District. The shrine's former Rank was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern typeing System.

The area surrounding the shrine is historically known as "Ogami no Shō," which referred to a {{ill|Manorialism|en|Manorialism|WD=Q1550557|lt=Manor|1=Manorialism}} (shōen). The {{ill|Shō River|en|Shō River|WD=Q7506083|lt=Shōgawa River|1=Shō River}} takes its name from this region. Historically, the river was also known as "Ogami River" due to its association with the shrine.

The primary deities enshrined are {{ill|Takaokami no Kami|ja|高龗神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaokami no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淤加美神|1=Kuraokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q644201}} and {{ill|Kuraokami|en|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|lt=Kuraokami|1=Kuraokami}}, both {{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|WD=Q3137449|lt=water god|1=Suijin}}, and {{ill|Seyorituhime|ja|瀬織津姫神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|1=Seoritsu-hime|2=ja|3=瀬織津姫|4=zh|5=瀨織津姬神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seyorituhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} is enshrined as a secondary deity.

== History ==

The exact date of the shrine's founding is unknown. Its earliest historical mention appears in the {{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|en|Nihon Kiryaku|WD=Q1053542|lt=Nihon Kiryaku|1=Nihon Kiryaku}}, where it is recorded that Ogami Shrine was elevated to the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank Upper|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank Upper|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} alongside {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}} and {{ill|Futagami Shrine|ja|二上神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futagami Shrine|1=Q45261123|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45261123}} on the 18th day of the 8th month in the 14th year of the {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|WD=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku era|1=Enryaku}} (795). Earlier references include a map stored in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} from {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|en|Tenpyō-hōji|WD=Q1346307|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|1=Tenpyō-hōji}} 3 (759), which details land distribution between this shrine and {{ill|Kenba Shrine|ja|荊波神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It is listed as one of the seven shrines of {{ill|Tonami District|ja|礪波郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonami District|1=Tonami district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588138}} in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}.

The name "Ogami" is theorized to mean "male deity," in contrast to "Himegami" (姫神, female deity), which is listed immediately before it in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}. However, the current deities of Ogami Shrine, Takaokami and Kuraokami, may have been associated with the shrine due to interpretations of its name.

Originally located near the Shōgawa River, flooding during the Kanbun era (1663) caused a new river channel to form between the main hall and the worship hall. In Hōei 7 (1710), the main hall was relocated to its current elevated position, and the remaining worship hall was repurposed as the {{ill|Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja|元雄神神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ogami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, {{ill|Uga Benzaiten|ja|宇賀弁才天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uga Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇賀神|1=Ugajin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q795080}} was enshrined at Moto-Ogami Shrine, which was subsequently renamed {{ill|Benzaiten Shrine|ja|弁財天社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}. The Benzaiten Shrine holds ceremonial openings once every 33 years, attracting many visitors.

During the medieval period, followers of the {{ill|Ikko-ikki|ja|一向宗徒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikko-ikki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} sect overthrew the ruling Ishiguro clan at Fukumi Castle, gaining control over the lands of {{ill|Takase Shrine|en|Takase Shrine|WD=Q3791906|lt=Takase Shrine|1=Takase Shrine (Nanto)}}. A portion of Takase Shrine’s clergy transitioned to Ogami Shrine. To this day, both shrines maintain close ties, and their head priest is shared.
